perturbation.original_text: The Fee shall be payable to Charity Tunes in two equal installments of $58,802.80 (plus applicable taxes) due to Charity Tunes on October 1, 2009 and November 1, 2009, respectively payable upon receipt of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Fee shall be payable to Charity Tunes in two equal installments of $58,802.80 (plus applicable taxes) due to Charity Tunes on October 1, 2010 and November 1, 2010, respectively payable upon receipt of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment dates to a year later when the agreement clearly states the promotion period occurs between October 1, 2009 to March 31, 2010 creates a contradiction, as the payment would be due after the promotional period.
perturbation.law_explanation: By setting the payment dates after the promotional period is completed, the contract becomes contradictory because there's no consideration being exchanged during the specified time. This could be interpreted as an impossibility of performance or a failure of consideration, potentially voiding the contract. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 261 addresses the concept of impossibility in contract law, stating that a party's duty to perform may be discharged if performance becomes impossible or impracticable due to unforeseen circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: impossibility | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you impossibility Impossibility is a defense from liability under criminal law and an excuse for non- performance under contract law . Under contract law, a party can raise an impossibility defense when an unforeseen event occurs after the contract is made which makes performance impossible. For example, if a person agrees to clean the local theater for a year but the local theater later burns down, the person is excused from performing the rest of the contract because the contract was predicated on the local theater’s existence. Under criminal law, impossibility is a defense in some jurisdictions which can remove liability for certain attempted crimes . Impossibility is typically split into two separate categories; factual and legal. Factual impossibility, or the inability to complete a crime due to a non-existent factual circumstance, does not function as a defense. Legal impossibility, or the belief that one’s actions are a crime when they actually are not, does function as a defense and can protect the defendant from attempted liability. [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE criminal law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-615. Excuse by Failure of Presupposed Conditions. Except so far as a seller may have assumed a greater obligation and subject to the preceding section on substituted performance: (a) Delay in delivery or non-delivery in whole or in part by a seller who complies with paragraphs (b) and (c) is not a breach of his duty under a contract for sale if performance as agreed has been made impracticable by the occurrence of a contingency the non-occurrence of which was a basic assumption on which the contract was made or by compliance in good faith with any applicable foreign or domestic governmental regulation or order whether or not it later proves to be invalid. (b) Where the causes mentioned in paragraph (a) affect only a part of the seller's capacity to perform, he must allocate production and deliveries among his customers but may at his option include regular customers not then under contract as well as his own requirements for further manufacture. He may so allocate in any manner which is fair and reasonable. (c) The seller must notify the buyer seasonably that there will be delay or non-delivery and, when allocation is required under paragraph (b), of the estimated quota thus made available for the buyer. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The contract modification, by altering the payment dates to after the promotional period, creates a legal contradiction. The original contract specified a promotional period from October 1, 2009, to March 31, 2010, with payments due in October and November 2009. The changed text shifts the payment dates to October and November 2010, which is after the promotional period. The LLM's law explanation indicates this could be seen as a failure of consideration, potentially voiding the contract, linking it to the concept of impossibility found in contract law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'impossibility' as 'an excuse for non- performance under contract law' when 'an unforeseen event occurs after the contract is made which makes performance impossible.' While the specific scenario is not 'unforeseen', the contradiction introduces a condition that makes the exchange of consideration during the agreed-upon promotional period impossible, thus directly contradicting the original terms and potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be given in writing and shall be delivered, either personally or by express delivery service, to the party to be notified. Notice to each party shall be deemed to have been duly given upon delivery, personally or by courier, addressed to the attention of the officer at the address set forth heretofore, or to such other officer or addresses as either party may designate, upon at least ten days written notice, to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be given verbally. Notice to each party shall be deemed to have been duly given upon utterance, addressed to any employee of the other party, at any location. There is no need for written notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for written notice and specifies that verbal notice is sufficient, contradicting the need for a written record of communication which can be a legal requirement.
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario's Statute of Frauds, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.19
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows verbal notice, which may violate the Statute of Frauds depending on the subject matter of the notice. Ontario's Statute of Frauds generally requires certain contracts, or modifications thereof, to be in writing to be enforceable. Some provinces have repealed statutes of frauds, but Ontario still has a Statute of Frauds that requires many contracts to be in writing.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the requirement for written notice with verbal notice, contradicting the Statute of Frauds, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.19, which generally requires certain contracts or interests to be created or assigned in writing. The original text ensures written records, while the changed text eliminates this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every estate or interest of freehold and every uncertain interest of, in, to or out of any messuages, lands, tenements or hereditaments shall be made or created by a writing signed by the parties making or creating the same.' Thus, the change introduces a clear non-compliance with the law, particularly if the agreement involves interests covered by the Statute of Frauds.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned by either party hereto without the written consent of the other but shall be binding upon the successors of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be assigned by either party without any consent. The agreement is not binding on the successors of the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for written consent for assignment and stating the agreement is not binding on successors directly contradicts standard contract law principles that protect the interests of all parties involved in the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the written consent clause violates established contract law principles related to assignment, which generally require mutual agreement to protect the interests of all parties. By making the agreement non-binding on successors the altered text conflicts with the principle that contractual obligations often extend to the successors of the original parties, unless otherwise specified and agreed upon. Section 317 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts addresses the concept of assignment of rights and delegation of duties, stating that, unless otherwise agreed, a contractual right can be assigned unless the assignment would materially change the duty of the obligor, materially increase the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, materially impair his chance of obtaining return performance, or materially reduce its value to him.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: assignment | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you assignment Assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another, known as the “ assignee .” This concept is used in both contract and property law. The term can refer to either the act of transfer or the rights /property/benefits being transferred. Contract Law Under contract law, assignment of a contract is both an assignment of rights and a delegation of duties in the absence of evidence otherwise. For example, if A contracts with B to teach B guitar for $50, A can assign this contract to C. That is, this assignment is both an assignment of A’s rights under the contract to the $50; and a delegation of A’s duty to teach guitar to C. In this example, A is both the “assignor” and the “delegee” who delegates the duties to another (C), C is known as the “ obligor ” who must perform the obligations to the assignee, and B is the “assignee” who is owed duties and is liable to the “obligor.” Assignment of Rights/Duties Under Contract Law There are a few notable rules regarding assignments under contract law. First, if an individual has not yet secured the contract to perform duties to another, he/she cannot assign his/her future right to an assignee. That is, if A has not yet contracted with B to teach B guitar, A cannot assign his/her rights to C. Second, rights cannot be assigned when they materially change the obligor’s duty and rights. Third, the obligor can sue the assignee directly if the assignee does not pay them. Following the previous example, this means that C (obligor) can sue B (assignee) if C teaches guitar to B, but B does not pay C $50 in return. Delegation of Duties If the promised performance requires a rare genius or skill, then the delegee cannot delegate it to the obligor. It can only be delegated if the promised performance is more commonplace. Further, an obligee can sue if the assignee does not perform. However, the delegee is secondarily liable unless there has been an express release of the delegee. That is, if B does want C to teach guitar but C refuses to, then B can sue C. If C still refuses to perform, then B can compel A to fulfill the duties under secondary liability. Lastly, a related concept is novation , which is when a new obligor substitutes and releases an old obligor. If novation occurs, then the original obligor’s duties are wiped out. However, novation requires an original obligee’s consent . Property Law Under property law, assignment typically arises in landlord-tenant situations. For example, A might be renting from landlord B but wants another party (C) to take over the property. In this scenario, A might be able to choose between assigning and subleasing the property to C. If assigning, A would be giving the entire balance of the term to C, with no reversion to anyone; whereas if subleasing, A would be giving the balance to C for a limited period of the remaining term. Under assignment, C would have privity of estate with the landlord, while under a sublease C would not. [Last reviewed in January of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law trusts inheritances & estates business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires written consent for assignment, protecting parties by ensuring mutual agreement. The changed text removes this consent requirement and states the agreement is not binding on successors, directly conflicting with standard contract law. The Wex snippet states that, under contract law, assignment of a contract is both an assignment of rights and a delegation of duties, implying that such assignments have legal implications for all parties, which is undermined by the changed text.
